per_sample.body: spec-adjacent-register — SPEC.md, `skills/**/SKILL.md`, SPEC-FORMAT.md, spec-referencing prose ! telegraph per telegraph skill; /sdd:explain decodes on demand.
per_sample.prose_minimal:
The skill named "spec-adjacent-register" applies to several categories of documents and content: the SPEC.md file, every SKILL.md file located anywhere within the skills directory tree, the SPEC-FORMAT.md file, and any prose that references specifications.

When writing in these contexts, use a compressed, telegraphic style of communication, following the conventions established by the telegraph skill. This means stripping out unnecessary words and writing in a dense, abbreviated manner similar to old telegraph messages.

Readers who need the content expanded back into normal, full prose can invoke the /sdd:explain command, which will decode the telegraphic writing into standard English on demand.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.1 — spec-adjacent-register

> V1: spec-adjacent-register — SPEC.md, `skills/**/SKILL.md`, SPEC-FORMAT.md, spec-referencing prose ! telegraph per telegraph skill; /sdd:explain decodes on demand.

In plain English: this invariant says that anything in the "spec-adjacent" set of files must be written in telegraph form — the compressed shorthand defined by the `telegraph` skill. That set is exactly four things: the top-level `SPEC.md`, every skill body under `skills/**/SKILL.md`, the `SPEC-FORMAT.md` contract, and any prose that talks about the spec. The trade-off is that telegraph is dense and hard to read cold, so the `/sdd:explain` command exists as the escape hatch: it decompresses any cited row back into plain English on demand. The "!" symbol here means "must" — these files don't get to opt out.

Cited interfaces:
- §I.format — `SPEC-FORMAT.md` is one of the files governed by this rule; it binds the row shape and section catalog that the telegraph form is written into.
- §I.explain — `/sdd:explain` is the command that decodes telegraph back into prose, which is exactly what is happening right now.

Tasks citing §V.1: none currently in the live §T section.

Bugs referencing §V.1: none.

Related invariants worth knowing:
- §V.2 draws the opposite-register boundary: GitHub-facing surfaces (README, issues, PRs, commit bodies) use "steno", not telegraph.
- §V.3 carves out exceptions inside telegraph text — code, paths, identifiers, quoted strings, etc. must never be compressed even when they sit inside a telegraph row.
- §V.11 splits the labour: SPEC-FORMAT.md owns *shape*, §V rows own *semantics*. So §V.1 states the rule; SPEC-FORMAT.md states what telegraph actually looks like on the page.

Bottom line: spec files and skill bodies are written in telegraph; humans who need prose run `/sdd:explain`.

## Hint

§V.1 is a foundational register rule with no live tasks or bugs attached, so the natural follow-ups are lateral — read the sibling register rule (§V.2) or the shape contract (§V.11) that pairs with it.

## Next

1. /sdd:explain §V.2 — read the opposite register rule (GitHub-facing → steno)
2. /sdd:explain §V.3 — read the verbatim-preservation carve-outs inside telegraph
3. /sdd:explain §V.11 — read how shape (SPEC-FORMAT.md) and semantics (§V) split
4. /sdd:explain §I.explain — read the prose-decoder interface contract
5. /sdd:explain --next — jump to the next pending §T row

per_sample.body: github-facing-register — README, issues, PRs, commit-msg bodies ! steno per steno skill; commit subjects = per-skill fixed templates, verbatim.
per_sample.prose_minimal: The github-facing-register specifies the conventions for text that appears in GitHub-facing contexts. README files, issues, pull requests, and the body portions of commit messages should be written in stenographic style, with the specific stenographic conventions varying according to the skill being documented or discussed, on a per-skill basis. Commit subject lines, however, are exempt from this stenographic treatment: they must follow fixed templates defined per skill, and those templates are to be reproduced verbatim without modification.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.2 — github-facing-register

> V2: github-facing-register — README, issues, PRs, commit-msg bodies ! steno per steno skill; commit subjects = per-skill fixed templates, verbatim.

In plain English: anything that surfaces on GitHub — the README, issue bodies, pull-request descriptions, and the body text of commit messages — must be written in the "steno" compressed register, following the rules set out in the steno skill. This is a deliberately different register from the telegraph form used inside SPEC.md and skill bodies (that one is governed by §V.1). Commit *subject lines* are exempt from the steno register: each skill defines a fixed subject template, and the skill must emit that template verbatim every time it commits, with no per-run rewording.

Cited siblings:

- The steno skill itself defines what "steno" compression looks like — it is the encoder for this register, just as the telegraph skill is the encoder for the SPEC-adjacent register cited in §V.1.
- §V.1 (spec-adjacent-register) is the sister rule: it governs SPEC.md and skill bodies under the *telegraph* register. Together §V.1 and §V.2 partition the writing surfaces into two distinct compressed registers.
- §V.3 (verbatim-preservation) constrains both registers — code, paths, identifiers, error strings, and other backtick-wrapped tokens are never compressed, even inside steno prose.
- §V.4 (symbol-set) notes that steno extends the shared symbol set with `&` and a list-`|`, which telegraph does not use.

No §T row is currently open against §V.2, and no §B bug cites it — the rule is in steady state.

Bottom line: GitHub-visible prose must be steno-encoded by the steno skill, but commit subjects bypass that and use frozen per-skill templates so machine-readable commit history stays uniform.

## Hint

§V.2 is a steady-state invariant — no open tasks, no bugs. Typical next moves are to read its sister rule (§V.1) for contrast, or jump to the steno skill that actually defines the encoding.

## Next

1. /sdd:explain §V.1 — read the sister telegraph-register rule
2. /sdd:explain §V.3 — read the verbatim-preservation constraint that overrides both registers
3. /sdd:explain §V.4 — read the shared symbol-set rule
4. /sdd:explain --next — jump to the next pending §T row
5. /sdd:check — audit whether GitHub-facing surfaces actually conform to §V.2

per_sample.body: verbatim-preservation — code, backticked text, paths, URLs, identifiers, numbers, versions, error strings, SQL, regex, JSON, YAML, quoted strings never compressed; backtick-wrapped tokens exempt every residue + cite audit.
per_sample.prose_minimal: The rule called verbatim-preservation requires that certain categories of content never be compressed, shortened, paraphrased, or otherwise altered. The categories that must be preserved exactly as written include: code, text wrapped in backticks, file paths, URLs, identifiers, numbers, version strings, error messages or error strings, SQL statements, regular expressions, JSON, YAML, and any quoted strings. Tokens that are wrapped in backticks are exempt from compression in every residue, and they must be cited in the audit.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.3 — verbatim-preservation

> V3: verbatim-preservation — code, backticked text, paths, URLs, identifiers, numbers, versions, error strings, SQL, regex, JSON, YAML, quoted strings never compressed; backtick-wrapped tokens exempt every residue + cite audit.

In plain English: when the spec or any spec-adjacent prose is written in telegraph form, certain kinds of content must be left exactly as-is — never abbreviated, never shortened, never paraphrased. The protected categories are: source code, anything wrapped in backticks, file paths, URLs, identifiers (variable names, function names, etc.), numeric values, version strings, error messages, SQL statements, regular expressions, JSON, YAML, and any quoted string. Additionally, anything wrapped in backticks gets a second privilege: it is exempted from the audits that check for leftover compression residue and from the audits that check that citations resolve. In other words, backticks act as a "do not touch, do not interpret" wrapper.

Cited siblings:

- §V.3 is referenced by no other §V row directly, but it is one of the four register-shaping invariants alongside §V.1 (spec-adjacent register uses telegraph), §V.2 (github-facing register uses steno), and §V.4 (symbol set). Together these four define what compression looks like and what it must leave alone.
- No live §T tasks cite §V.3 — the rule is enforced through the mechanical audit script (see §V.40) rather than through a pending task.
- No live §B bugs cite §V.3 — no recorded recurrence has stemmed from a verbatim-preservation failure.

Why this matters: telegraph compression is a real risk to correctness. If a path like `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` got shortened, or a version number got rounded, or an error string got paraphrased, the spec would diverge from the executable reality of the code. §V.3 draws a hard line: compression is for natural-language prose only; anything that has to match a machine or a tool stays byte-exact. The backtick exemption from residue and cite audits is the practical escape hatch — it lets the spec quote literal text that might otherwise look like a stale citation or a compression artifact.

Bottom line: in telegraph-form prose, leave machine-meaningful tokens alone; use backticks as the universal "this is literal, hands off" marker.

## Hint

§V.3 is a live invariant with no pending tasks or open bugs against it — it is currently being upheld. Item 1 lets you read the adjacent register-shaping invariants for context; item 2 audits whether the rule is actually holding in the current tree.

## Next

1. /sdd:explain §V.1 — read the sibling spec-adjacent-register invariant in prose
2. /sdd:check — audit whether verbatim-preservation still holds across the repo
3. /sdd:explain --next — read the next pending §T row

per_sample.body: symbol-set — keep set `→ ≥ ≤ ! ? §` (steno adds `&`, list-`|`); heavier math operators ! ASCII words.
per_sample.prose_minimal: Keep the symbol set consisting of the arrow, greater-than-or-equal-to sign, less-than-or-equal-to sign, exclamation mark, question mark, and section sign. In stenographic style, also add the ampersand and the pipe character used for lists. Heavier mathematical operators should not be written as ASCII words.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.4 — symbol-set

> V4: symbol-set — keep set `→ ≥ ≤ ! ? §` (steno adds `&`, list-`|`); heavier math operators ! ASCII words.

In plain English: telegraph and steno writing across this repo share a small, fixed set of symbols. The telegraph register (used in SPEC.md and skill bodies) is allowed to use exactly six symbols: the arrow `→`, the comparison operators `≥` and `≤`, the imperative/strong-assertion marker `!`, the question mark `?`, and the section sigil `§`. The steno register (used in GitHub-facing prose per §V.2) gets two more: the ampersand `&` and the pipe `|` for list separation. Anything heavier — for example mathematical operators like ∑, ∀, ∈, ⊂, ⊕, or ≈ — must be written out as ASCII words instead of pulled in as new symbols.

Cited siblings:

- §V.1 (spec-adjacent-register) — defines the telegraph register that consumes the base six symbols: SPEC.md, `skills/**/SKILL.md`, SPEC-FORMAT.md, and spec-referencing prose must all be written in telegraph.
- §V.2 (github-facing-register) — defines the steno register that gets the extra `&` and list-`|`: README, issues, PRs, and commit-message bodies. Commit subjects are exempt (fixed per-skill templates).
- §V.3 (verbatim-preservation) — the counterweight: even within these registers, code, backticked tokens, paths, URLs, identifiers, numbers, error strings, SQL, regex, JSON, YAML, and quoted strings are never compressed and never symbol-substituted.

No §T tasks or §B bugs currently cite §V.4 directly — the invariant is stable and being upheld.

Bottom line: the project deliberately constrains itself to a tiny shared symbol vocabulary so that telegraph and steno stay readable and decodable; reaching for a fancier math or logic symbol is a violation, and the correct move is to spell the concept out in ASCII words.

## Hint

§V.4 is a stable invariant with no open tasks or bugs against it. Typical follow-ups are reading its sibling register-definition invariants (§V.1, §V.2, §V.3), or auditing whether the live spec actually respects the symbol set.

## Next

1. /sdd:explain §V.1 — read the telegraph-register invariant in prose
2. /sdd:explain §V.2 — read the steno-register invariant in prose
3. /sdd:explain §V.3 — read the verbatim-preservation counterweight in prose
4. /sdd:explain --next — jump to the next pending §T row
5. /sdd:check — audit whether the symbol set is actually being respected across the repo

per_sample.body: sole-source-of-truth — SPEC.md @ repo root is sole live spec; no docs/ tree, no sidecars; SPEC.archive.md sibling carries immutable archived rows only.
per_sample.prose_minimal: SPEC.md, located at the root of the repository, is the sole source of truth and the only live specification document. There is no documentation tree (no docs/ directory) and there are no sidecar files associated with it. A sibling file named SPEC.archive.md exists alongside SPEC.md, and that file carries only immutable archived rows.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.10 — sole source of truth

> V10: sole-source-of-truth — SPEC.md @ repo root is sole live spec; no docs/ tree, no sidecars; SPEC.archive.md sibling carries immutable archived rows only.

In plain English: there is exactly one live specification file for this plugin, and it lives at `SPEC.md` in the repository root. No other directory (like `docs/`) and no sidecar files are allowed to hold live spec content. A second file, `SPEC.archive.md`, may sit next to it, but only to hold rows that have been retired or archived — once a row lands there it is frozen and never edited again.

Cited siblings: none — this row stands alone, but it underpins several others.

Tasks citing this invariant: none directly; the spec-write skills (see §V.20 write-ownership) enforce it operationally.

Related invariants worth knowing (not formally cited from §V.10, but adjacent):
- §V.11 — `SPEC-FORMAT.md` binds row shape and section catalog; semantics live in §V rows. So format and semantics are split across two files, but only `SPEC.md` is the live source of truth for what the system *means*.
- §V.16 — archive semantics: archived §T/§B and retired §V rows migrate verbatim into `SPEC.archive.md`, stay cite-resolvable, and are never edited. This is the read-side counterpart to §V.10's write-side rule.
- §V.20 — write-ownership: `/sdd:spec` is the sole author of `SPEC.md`, which is how §V.10's "sole live spec" rule is actually enforced day to day.

Bottom line: if you are looking for the truth about how this plugin should behave, read `SPEC.md` at the repo root — nothing else is authoritative, and anything in `SPEC.archive.md` is history, not policy.

## Hint

§V.10 is a foundational invariant with no direct citers in §T or §B, so the natural next moves are sideways — into the invariants that operationalize it (§V.16 archive, §V.20 write-ownership) or into the format contract (§V.11).

## Next

1. /sdd:explain §V.11 — read the shape/semantics split that complements §V.10
2. /sdd:explain §V.16 — read how archived rows behave under §V.10's sidecar rule
3. /sdd:explain §V.20 — read who is actually allowed to write `SPEC.md`
4. /sdd:explain --next — jump to the next pending §T row instead
5. /sdd:check — audit whether the repo currently honors §V.10

per_sample.body: shape-semantics-split — SPEC-FORMAT.md binds row shape + section catalog + citation grammar; §V rows bind semantics + enforcement; neither restates the other.
per_sample.prose_minimal: The shape-semantics-split refers to a structural arrangement in which the document SPEC-FORMAT.md is responsible for binding together the row shape, the section catalog, and the citation grammar, while the rows found in section five are responsible for binding together the semantics and the enforcement. Under this arrangement, neither side restates what the other side already covers.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.11 — shape-semantics-split

> V11: shape-semantics-split — SPEC-FORMAT.md binds row shape + section catalog + citation grammar; §V rows bind semantics + enforcement; neither restates the other.

In plain English: there's a clean division of labor between two files. `SPEC-FORMAT.md` defines the *form* of the spec — what a row looks like, which sections exist, and how citations are written. The `§V` invariants in `SPEC.md` define the *meaning* — what each rule actually requires and how it's enforced. Neither file should repeat what the other says: the format file never tries to explain what an invariant means, and an invariant row never re-describes the row syntax it happens to use.

Cited interfaces:
- §I.format — `SPEC-FORMAT.md` is the contract for row shape + section catalog, loaded by the spec, check, compact, and reorganize skills. This is the file §V.11 places on the "shape" side of the split.

Related invariants (not cited, but adjacent):
- §V.10 (sole-source-of-truth) puts `SPEC.md` at the repo root as the live spec. §V.11 then says: within that authoritative file, semantics live in `§V` rows; shape lives elsewhere in `SPEC-FORMAT.md`.
- §V.40 (mechanical-realization) makes a parallel split on the audit side: skill bodies + `SPEC-FORMAT.md` state contracts, and `scripts/check-mechanical.py` realizes them — no duplication.

Bottom line: `SPEC-FORMAT.md` owns syntax; `§V` rows own semantics. If you find yourself describing row shape inside an invariant, or describing a rule's intent inside the format file, you've violated §V.11.

## Hint

§V.11 is an active structural invariant — `run 1` walks to the next pending task, `run 2` reads the format contract it pairs with, `run 3` audits whether the split has actually held.

## Next

1. /sdd:explain --next — read the next pending §T row
2. /sdd:explain §I.format — decompress the SPEC-FORMAT.md interface row
3. /sdd:check — audit whether shape/semantics duplication has crept in

per_sample.body: monotonic-numbering — V/T/B ids strictly increasing in section order; gaps OK, reuse banned; sole renumber path = /sdd:reorganize (map append + cite sweep, same commit).
per_sample.prose_minimal: The system enforces a monotonic numbering rule for identifiers of types V, T, and B. These identifiers must appear in strictly increasing order as you move through the sections in sequence. Gaps between identifier numbers are permitted and acceptable, but reusing any identifier that has previously been assigned is forbidden. The only sanctioned way to renumber these identifiers is through the /sdd:reorganize operation, which performs two actions—appending to the mapping and conducting a citation sweep—both of which must occur within the same commit.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.12 — monotonic-numbering

> V12: monotonic-numbering — V/T/B ids strictly increasing in section order; gaps OK, reuse banned; sole renumber path = /sdd:reorganize (map append + cite sweep, same commit).

In plain English: this invariant governs how row identifiers (V-numbers, T-numbers, B-numbers) behave across the spec. The rule has three parts:

1. **Strictly increasing in section order.** Within each section (§V, §T, §B), as you read top to bottom, the numeric ids must always go up — never sideways, never down. V1 is followed by V2 or V10 or V12, but never by V11 once V12 has appeared.

2. **Gaps are fine, reuse is forbidden.** You can jump from V4 to V10 to V20 — those gaps are deliberate (they leave room for clustering related rows by topic, as the §V section preamble notes). But once an id has been used, even if the row is later retired or archived, that number can never be reassigned to a different row.

3. **Renumbering only happens through one channel.** If ids ever need to be reshuffled — for instance, to re-cluster rows after the spec grows — the only legitimate path is the `/sdd:reorganize` skill. That skill must do two things in the same commit: append the old→new mapping to `.claude/spec-renumber-map.json`, and sweep every citation in the spec to point at the new ids.

Tasks citing §V.12:
- None currently. No §T row in the live spec cites V12 directly.

Bugs citing §V.12:
- None. No §B row references V12.

Related invariants (referenced by V12's machinery, not cited by V12 itself):
- §V.15 — renumber-chain-walk: describes how `.claude/spec-renumber-map.json` is consumed (append-only, walked newest-first) to resolve historical ids to live ones or to an `archive` sentinel. V12 produces the map entries; V15 governs how they're read.
- §V.16 — archive-semantics: defines where retired §V rows go (SPEC.archive.md) once they leave the live numbering sequence.

Bottom line: id numbers in SPEC.md only ever grow, never repeat, and only `/sdd:reorganize` may rewrite them — and only by atomically updating both the rename map and every citer.

## Hint

§V.12 is a live invariant with no current task or bug pressure on it. Typical next moves are item 1 to read the closely-related renumber-chain-walk rule, or item 2 to see the archive destination for retired ids.

## Next

1. /sdd:explain §V.15 — read the renumber-chain-walk rule that consumes the rename map
2. /sdd:explain §V.16 — read the archive-semantics rule for retired rows
3. /sdd:explain --next — jump to the next pending §T row
4. /sdd:check — audit whether live ids actually satisfy V12

per_sample.body: cite-resolution — every cite ! resolve: `cites` tokens → live/archived V/T/B row or live §I kind, `fix` tokens → §V row, free-text `§<S>.<n>` → §<S> row; renumber sweeps citers same commit.
per_sample.prose_minimal: When citing, every citation must be resolved. The `cites` tokens need to point to either a live or archived row in V, T, or B, or to a live section I entry of the appropriate kind. The `fix` tokens must point to a row in section V. Free-text references in the form `§<S>.<n>` must point to a row in section S. When a renumbering occurs, all citers must be updated to use the new numbers within the same commit as the renumbering.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.13 — cite-resolution

> V13: cite-resolution — every cite ! resolve: `cites` tokens → live/archived V/T/B row or live §I kind, `fix` tokens → §V row, free-text `§<S>.<n>` → §<S> row; renumber sweeps citers same commit.

In plain English: every citation written anywhere in SPEC.md must point to something real. The rules differ slightly by where the citation appears:

- A token in a `cites` field (the last column of a §T task row or other row that lists what it depends on) must resolve either to a live or archived §V, §T, or §B row, or to a live §I interface kind.
- A token in a `fix` field (the last column of a §B bug row) must resolve to a §V invariant row — bugs always get pinned to the invariant they violated.
- A free-form prose reference of the form `§<Section>.<n>` (for example `§V.27` written inside another row's body) must resolve to a row in that section.

The second clause ties this to renumbering: whenever ids shift, every place that cites the old id must be updated in the same commit that does the renumbering. No half-swept state is ever allowed to land.

Cited siblings:

- §V.12 (monotonic-numbering) — establishes that the only legitimate way ids can change is through `/sdd:reorganize`, which is the operation that must carry the cite sweep along with it.
- §V.15 (renumber-chain-walk) — explains how a historical id is resolved through the append-only `.claude/spec-renumber-map.json` map; this is the mechanism that lets "live/archived" resolution actually work after renumbers have happened.
- §V.16 (archive-semantics) — confirms that archived rows in `SPEC.archive.md` stay cite-resolvable, which is what makes the "live or archived" clause meaningful.

Tasks that cite §V.13: none currently. This invariant is enforced today by the mechanical script (see §V.40) rather than by an in-flight task.

Bugs against §V.13: none recorded.

Bottom line: a citation that doesn't resolve is a spec defect, and a renumber that leaves stale citers behind is also a spec defect — both are caught against this invariant in the same commit that introduces the change.

## Hint

§V.13 is a live invariant, not a task — there is nothing to "build." Useful next steps are reading the closely related renumber-chain rules, or running an audit to see whether any citations in the current SPEC.md actually fail to resolve.

## Next

2. /sdd:explain §V.12 — read the monotonic-numbering rule that constrains when ids may shift
3. /sdd:check — audit live SPEC.md for unresolved or stale citations
4. /sdd:explain --next — jump to the next pending §T row

per_sample.body: pinned-cite-ban — PUBLISHED bodies ! placeholder (`§V.<n>`) or named-invariant form, never pinned §-digit cites; SPEC.md-narrative + REPO-LOCAL pinned cites ! resolve live.
per_sample.prose_minimal: In published bodies of work, citations must never use pinned section-digit references; instead, they must appear either as a placeholder in the form §V.<n> or as a named-invariant form. In contrast, within the SPEC.md narrative and repository-local contexts, pinned citations are permitted and should resolve live.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.14 — pinned-cite-ban

> V14: pinned-cite-ban — PUBLISHED bodies ! placeholder (`§V.<n>`) or named-invariant form, never pinned §-digit cites; SPEC.md-narrative + REPO-LOCAL pinned cites ! resolve live.

In plain English: this invariant governs how cross-references to invariants are written, and the rule depends on *where* the reference appears. In files that get published (the marketplace-visible plugin source — skills, READMEs, anything shipped to consuming repos), you must never write a concrete pinned citation like `§V.14`. Instead, use either a literal placeholder (the string `§V.<n>` with the angle brackets) or the named form (e.g. "pinned-cite-ban"). The reason: published bodies travel forward through time, but invariant numbers can shift under renumbering, so a pinned digit there will silently rot. In contrast, citations *inside* SPEC.md's own narrative and inside REPO-LOCAL files (the `.claude/` cache, CLAUDE.md, the repo's own README) *are* allowed to pin digits — but those pinned digits must resolve to a live row at audit time, not a retired or missing one.

Related scope set:

- §V.42 — defines the scope vocabulary this invariant rides on: PUBLISHED (marketplace source dirs), REPO-LOCAL (`.claude/**`, README.md, CLAUDE.md), SPEC-ADJACENT (SPEC.md, skill bodies, SPEC-FORMAT.md). §V.14 carves PUBLISHED out as the no-pin zone and leaves the other two as resolve-live zones.

Related renumber machinery:

- §V.12 — monotonic-numbering says ids are append-only and reuse is banned, with `/sdd:reorganize` as the sole renumber path. §V.14 exists because §V.12 permits renumbering at all: if renumbers can happen, pinned cites in long-lived published bodies are landmines.
- §V.15 — renumber-chain-walk describes how `.claude/spec-renumber-map.json` lets historical ids resolve newest-first. This is what makes "must resolve live" in §V.14 mechanically possible for the REPO-LOCAL side.
- §V.13 — cite-resolution defines what "resolve" means for any cite token. §V.14 adds a *form* restriction (no pinned digits in PUBLISHED) on top of §V.13's *target* restriction (must point at a real row).

No §T rows currently cite §V.14. No §B bug has been logged against it.

Bottom line: in anything that ships, write `§V.<n>` or the invariant's name; in SPEC.md prose and `.claude/` files, pinned digits are fine but must still hit a live row.

## Hint

§V.14 is a live invariant, not a task — there's nothing to "do" on it directly. Typical next moves are auditing whether the rule currently holds (item 1), reading the scope vocabulary it depends on (item 2), or reading the renumber machinery that motivates it (item 3).

per_sample.body: renumber-chain-walk — `.claude/spec-renumber-map.json` append-only; historical id resolves newest-first to live id or `archive` sentinel (→ SPEC.archive.md §V.retired block, never live row).
per_sample.prose_minimal: The renumber-chain-walk mechanism operates on a file located at `.claude/spec-renumber-map.json`, which is maintained in an append-only fashion, meaning entries are added over time but never removed or modified in place. When resolving a historical identifier, the resolution process walks through the map starting from the newest entries and moving backward toward older ones, in that order. The resolution will yield one of two possible results: either it maps to a live identifier currently in use, or it maps to a special sentinel value called `archive`. When the resolution produces the `archive` sentinel, this directs the lookup to the retired block in section V of the `SPEC.archive.md` file. Under no circumstances does a historical identifier resolve to a live row when the archive sentinel is involved; the archive sentinel always points exclusively to the retired block and never to an active, live row.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.15 — renumber-chain-walk

> V15: renumber-chain-walk — `.claude/spec-renumber-map.json` append-only; historical id resolves newest-first to live id or `archive` sentinel (→ SPEC.archive.md §V.retired block, never live row).

In plain English: when a §V row gets renumbered (by `/sdd:reorganize`), the old id doesn't just vanish — it gets recorded in a small JSON file at `.claude/spec-renumber-map.json`. That file is append-only: entries are added, never edited or deleted. When something later tries to look up an old id (e.g. a stale citation in a commit message or an external reference), the resolver walks the map newest-entry-first, following the chain of renames until it lands on one of two outcomes: either a currently-live id in SPEC.md, or the sentinel value `archive`. The sentinel means the row was retired and now lives in the `§V.retired` block of `SPEC.archive.md` — it must never resolve back to a live SPEC.md row.

Cited siblings (rows that reference §V.15):

This invariant is not directly cited by any §T task or §B bug in the live spec, but it sits in a tight cluster with:

- §V.12 (monotonic-numbering) — establishes that ids strictly increase and that `/sdd:reorganize` is the sole renumber path. §V.15 is the mechanism that keeps old ids resolvable after such a renumber.
- §V.13 (cite-resolution) — requires every cite to resolve to a live or archived row. §V.15 provides the walk procedure that makes old `§V.<n>` cites still resolve.
- §V.14 (pinned-cite-ban) — bans pinned numeric cites in PUBLISHED bodies precisely because numbers can shift; §V.15 handles the cases where pinned cites legitimately exist in SPEC.md narrative or REPO-LOCAL files.
- §V.16 (archive-semantics) — defines the destination the `archive` sentinel points to (SPEC.archive.md `§V.retired` block) and confirms archived rows stay cite-resolvable.

The `/sdd:explain` skill itself reads this map during LOAD step 3 — when given a `§V.<n>` argument, it walks the chain newest-first to land on the current row before rendering.

Bottom line: old §V ids never become dead links; an append-only map lets any historical citation resolve forward to today's live row or to a clearly marked archived row, and that walk is the contract every cite-resolving skill relies on.

## Hint

§V.15 is a live invariant — it isn't pending work and has no open §T or §B against it. Useful next steps are reading its immediate neighbors (§V.12, §V.13, §V.16) to see the full renumber/cite/archive contract, or auditing whether code actually honors the chain walk.

## Next

1. /sdd:explain §V.12 — read the monotonic-numbering rule that §V.15 supports
2. /sdd:explain §V.13 — read the cite-resolution contract that depends on the chain walk
3. /sdd:explain §V.16 — read the archive-semantics rule that defines the `archive` sentinel target
4. /sdd:explain --next — jump to the next pending §T row
5. /sdd:check — audit whether the renumber map and chain walk are honored in code

per_sample.body: x|add CI: script --self-test + audit dirty-verdict gate on push/PR|V40,V62
per_sample.prose_minimal: The task is to add Continuous Integration (CI) for the project. The CI should run a script that performs a self-test along with an audit, and it should enforce a "dirty-verdict" gate, meaning the build or check should fail (or be blocked) if the audit returns a dirty verdict. This gate should be triggered on both push events and pull request (PR) events. The relevant version or requirement identifiers associated with this task are V40 and V62.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §T.1 — add CI: script self-test + audit dirty-verdict gate on push/PR

> T1|x|add CI: script --self-test + audit dirty-verdict gate on push/PR|V40,V62

In plain English: this task added a continuous-integration check that runs on every push and pull request. The CI job runs the mechanical audit script's built-in self-test, then runs the audit itself and fails the build if the audit reports any "dirty" verdict against the spec.

Cited invariants:

- §V.40 — mechanical-realization: deterministic audit rules live in exactly one place, `scripts/check-mechanical.py`. Skill bodies and SPEC-FORMAT.md state the contracts but never duplicate parser pseudo-code, and per-run regex paraphrases are banned. The CI gate enforces this by running that single script as the authority on what counts as drift.
- §V.62 — tooling-preference: pattern scans use `rg --pcre2`, JSON parsing uses `jq` with python3 fallback, and the audit core is single-file stdlib-only python3. Frontmatter grants must be the narrowest pattern that fits actual use; zero-body-use grants are banned; script-sole-use interpreter grants pin the script path. Because `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` does not expand inside frontmatter, broad grants get an inline note citing that upstream limitation. The CI job exercises this tooling stack — invoking python3 against the pinned script path — and so depends on these rules holding.

Status: complete (`x`).

Bottom line: §V.40 (one mechanical script is the sole source of audit truth) and §V.62 (constrained tooling and grant patterns) are now backed by an automated push/PR gate that runs the script's self-test plus the audit and rejects any dirty verdict.

## Hint

§T.1 is closed — it is historical context, not pending work. `run 1` moves to the next pending task in §T; `run 2` expands one of the invariants this task cited; `run 3` audits whether the CI gate still actually enforces §V.40 + §V.62 in the current code.

per_sample.body: x|add script `emit-overview` mode (§G/§C/§I/§T/§B bodies + §V id list, no §V bodies); check LOAD step 1 → emit-overview i/o whole-file Read|V64,V40
per_sample.prose_minimal:
The user wants the following changes implemented.

First, the user is asking to add a new mode to the script called `emit-overview`. In this mode, the script should emit the bodies of sections §G, §C, §I, §T, and §B in full. For section §V, the script should emit only the list of identifiers from §V, but it should not emit the bodies of the §V entries themselves.

Second, the user wants the LOAD step 1 procedure to be checked and updated so that the `emit-overview` mode is used for input and output instead of performing a Read of the whole file.

The items affected by this change, or the tags associated with this request, are V64 and V40.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §T.4 — add script `emit-overview` mode; route check LOAD step 1 through it

```
T4|x|add script `emit-overview` mode (§G/§C/§I/§T/§B bodies + §V id list, no §V bodies); check LOAD step 1 → emit-overview i/o whole-file Read|V64,V40
```

In plain English: this task added a new mode to `scripts/check-mechanical.py` called `emit-overview`. When invoked, the script prints the full bodies of sections §G (goal), §C (constraints), §I (interfaces), §T (tasks), and §B (bugs), plus just the *ids* of §V invariant rows — but not the §V bodies themselves. The `/sdd:check` skill's first LOAD step was then changed to call this mode instead of reading the entire `SPEC.md` file. The whole point: stop pulling §V bodies into context at the top of every check run, since they get fetched later on demand via `emit-v-slices`.

Cited invariants:

- §V.64 — single-load. §V row bodies are only allowed to enter run context via the script's `emit-v-slices` mode. Reading the whole `SPEC.md` is banned wherever an `emit` mode covers the need; full reads are reserved for operator-driven rewrite sweeps like `/sdd:compact` and `/sdd:reorganize`. This task is the realization of that rule for `/sdd:check`.
- §V.40 — mechanical-realization. Deterministic audit logic lives exactly once, inside `scripts/check-mechanical.py`. Skill bodies and `SPEC-FORMAT.md` describe the contract but never duplicate the parser. Adding `emit-overview` to the script (rather than baking section-extraction logic into the check skill) honors this.

Related bug:

- §B.6 — the original double-load: `/sdd:check` LOAD step 1 read the whole `SPEC.md`, then step 4 called `emit-v-slices` and pulled §V bodies a second time. On a large spec, step 1 alone could hit the Read tool's pagination cap. §V.64 was the invariant tightened in response, and §T.4 is the task that actually closes the loop.

Status: complete (`x`).

Bottom line: §T.4 is the work that made §V.64 real for `/sdd:check` — a new script mode emits everything except §V bodies, so the check skill never has to slurp `SPEC.md` whole.

## Hint

§T.4 is closed and its cited invariant (§V.64) plus the bug it fixed (§B.6) form a complete story. `run 1` walks forward to the next pending task; `run 2` or `run 3` deepens this thread by expanding the cited invariant or the originating bug in prose.

per_sample.body: x|sweep: add PROGRESS § to multi-phase recipes — scope vocab {check, build, compact, reorganize}; per skill TaskCreate per recipe phase, TaskUpdate per transition, frontmatter allowed-tools += `TaskCreate`, `TaskUpdate`|V24,V62
per_sample.prose_minimal: The sweep should add a PROGRESS section to multi-phase recipes. The scope vocabulary for this section consists of the terms check, build, compact, and reorganize. For each skill, a TaskCreate should be issued per recipe phase, and a TaskUpdate should be issued per transition between phases. The frontmatter's allowed-tools field should be extended to include TaskCreate and TaskUpdate. This applies to V24 and V62.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §T.5 — sweep: add PROGRESS § to multi-phase recipes

> T5|x|sweep: add PROGRESS § to multi-phase recipes — scope vocab {check, build, compact, reorganize}; per skill TaskCreate per recipe phase, TaskUpdate per transition, frontmatter allowed-tools += `TaskCreate`, `TaskUpdate`|V24,V62

In plain English: this task was a one-shot sweep across the four multi-phase recipe skills — `check`, `build`, `compact`, and `reorganize` — to add a "PROGRESS" section that drives a live, in-UI checklist while the recipe runs. For each phase of a given recipe, the skill issues a `TaskCreate` call at the start and a `TaskUpdate` call to mark the phase `in_progress` and then `completed` as it transitions. To make those calls legal, each skill's frontmatter `allowed-tools` list had to grow two entries: `TaskCreate` and `TaskUpdate`. The scope was explicitly limited to that four-skill vocabulary — single-phase skills were left alone.

Cited invariants:

- **§V.24 — response-shape.** Every user-typeable skill output must end with a `## Next` block (1–5 atomic items, no `Reply` prefix, positional dispatch like `run <int>` or `run /<plugin>:<cmd> [args]`), optionally preceded by a `## Hint` of at most 3 lines. Crucially for this task, multi-phase runs in the set `{check, build --all, compact, reorganize}` *must* additionally emit a live harness checklist — one `TaskCreate` per recipe phase at the start, then `TaskUpdate` transitions from `in_progress` to `completed`. That checklist is ephemeral harness UI: it never persists as repo state and never replaces the REPORT or the `## Next` block. §T.5 is the row that actually wired that requirement into the four skills.

- **§V.62 — tooling-preference.** This invariant governs what tools a skill is allowed to invoke and how its frontmatter grants must be written: pattern scans prefer `rg --pcre2`, JSON parsing prefers `jq` with a `python3` fallback, audit core stays single-file stdlib-only `python3`, and frontmatter grants must be the narrowest pattern that covers actual body usage — zero-body-use grants are banned. §T.5 touches this invariant because expanding `allowed-tools` to include `TaskCreate` and `TaskUpdate` is exactly the kind of frontmatter change V62 polices: the grants are only legal if the skill body actually issues those calls, which the PROGRESS § ensures.

Status: complete (`x`).

Bottom line: §T.5 added the PROGRESS-checklist machinery (per-phase `TaskCreate` + `TaskUpdate`) to the four multi-phase recipe skills and widened their `allowed-tools` to match, satisfying §V.24's live-harness requirement under §V.62's narrowest-grant rule.

## Hint

§T.5 is closed and historical. `run 1` moves to the next pending task; `run 2` or `run 3` reads the cited invariants in prose; `run 4` audits whether the PROGRESS § still matches what the skills actually do.

## Next

3. /sdd:explain §V.62 — expand the tooling-preference invariant in prose
4. /sdd:check — audit whether the closed sweep still holds in the skill bodies

per_sample.body: x|script audit emits batch-advisory row from §V row count + PUBLISHED file census; check batch step 1 consumes row, retire hand-computed heuristic|V46,V40
per_sample.prose_minimal: The script audit produces a batch-advisory row that is derived from the row count in section five (specifically §V) combined with a census of the files that have been published. The check batch process, at its first step, should consume this advisory row directly. The hand-computed heuristic that was previously used for this purpose should be retired. This work is tracked under references V46 and V40.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §T.6 — script audit emits batch-advisory row from §V row count + PUBLISHED file census

```
T6|x|script audit emits batch-advisory row from §V row count + PUBLISHED file census; check batch step 1 consumes row, retire hand-computed heuristic|V46,V40
```

In plain English: this task taught `scripts/check-mechanical.py` to compute the recommended parallel-agent count for §V classification itself — based on how many §V rows exist and how many PUBLISHED files there are — and emit that count as an advisory row in the audit output. The `/sdd:check` skill then reads that row to decide batching, instead of the LLM eyeballing the numbers and doing the arithmetic in its head.

Cited invariants:

- §V.46 — §V classification may run in parallel, but the agent count must come from a script-emitted row of the form `batch|ADVISORY|recommended: <n> agents`. The formula is `ceil(|V|/15)` clamped to `[1, 4]`, with a narrow-scope override: if the PUBLISHED file census is less than `ceil(|V|/2)`, force 1 agent. The LLM is forbidden from computing this itself; batches must be contiguous spans and use the canonical prompt block verbatim. Failed batches re-run serially. This row closes §B.7.
- §V.40 — deterministic audit rules are realized once, in `scripts/check-mechanical.py`. Skill bodies and SPEC-FORMAT describe contracts but never duplicate parser pseudo-code, and per-run regex paraphrase is banned. So the batch-count formula belongs in the script, not in the skill body.

Status: complete (`x`).

Bottom line: the batch-agent count for §V classification is now a script output, not an LLM judgment — satisfying §V.46's "LLM never hand-computes count" clause and §V.40's "rules realized once in script" clause, and closing the §B.7 regression where the override was checked too late.

per_sample.prose_minimal: Initialize the file located at `skills/monitor/SKILL.md`. This initialization should set up automatic firing of deviation capture according to the monitor protocol. The trigger for this auto-firing must be defined only in the frontmatter description, and the bodies of any existing skills must remain byte-identical (unchanged at the byte level). per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §T.7 — init monitor skill

> T7|x|init `skills/monitor/SKILL.md`: auto-fire deviation capture per monitor-protocol; trigger in frontmatter description only, existing skill bodies byte-identical|V65,V61

In plain English: this task created a new skill at `skills/monitor/SKILL.md` whose job is to auto-fire whenever the plugin detects that a consumer repo is deviating from one of its skills (wrong version, unexpected behavior, etc.). The skill is wired so that its auto-fire trigger lives only in the frontmatter `description` field, and the bodies of every other already-shipped skill were left byte-for-byte identical — i.e. adding monitor did not require editing the other skills.

Cited invariants:

- §V.65 — monitor-protocol. When a consumer-repo skill deviates, the monitor skill must capture the skill name, version, and expected-vs-actual behavior, redact any consumer paths/code/identifiers before publishing, dedup against existing GitHub issues with `gh issue list` (commenting instead of filing duplicates), gate every `gh` write through AskUserQuestion while showing the resolved `--repo` target, and assert that the gh-write target equals the manifest `.repository` field immediately before the write so a repo named in the deviation excerpt cannot bleed into the `<target>`. If the current working directory is the plugin repo itself, it must hand off to the backprop flow (§V.27) and file no issue.
- §V.61 — sub-skill flags. Auto-fire sub-skills (telegraph, backprop, socratic, steno, **monitor**) must declare `user-invocable: false` in their frontmatter, and must never use `disable-model-invocation: true` — that flag hides the skill from the Skill tool, which would defeat the auto-fire purpose.

Status: complete (`x`).

Bottom line: the monitor skill exists and auto-fires per §V.65, with the §V.61 frontmatter discipline (`user-invocable: false`, no `disable-model-invocation`), and shipping it did not touch any other skill body.

per_sample.body: x|script: admit MATCH as clean verdict on §I rows, per-row-type vocab validation + self-tests|V43,V40
per_sample.prose_minimal: The script should admit MATCH as a clean verdict on the rows in section one. It should perform per-row-type vocabulary validation along with self-tests. This applies to versions forty-three and forty.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §T.8 — admit MATCH as clean verdict on §I rows

> T8|x|script: admit MATCH as clean verdict on §I rows, per-row-type vocab validation + self-tests|V43,V40

In plain English: this task taught `scripts/check-mechanical.py` to recognize `MATCH` as a legitimate clean verdict — but only when it appears on a §I (interface) row. It also added per-row-type vocabulary validation, so the script now checks that each verdict is admissible for the kind of row it's attached to (a §V row can't carry `MATCH`, a §I row can't carry a §V-only verdict, etc.). Self-tests were added to lock the rules in.

Cited invariants:

- §V.43 — drift-verdict-vocab. This is the master list of admissible verdicts: dirty `{VIOLATE, UNVERIFIABLE, UNRESOLVED, TYPE-MISMATCH, DRIFT, MISSING, STALE, EXTRA}`, silent `{HOLD, HOLD-SINCE-CLEAN, SCOPE-EMPTY, LATENT}`, surfaced-clean `{VIOLATE-CAPTURED}`, and — added by this task — §I-clean `{MATCH}` restricted to §I rows. The invariant pins the rule that the script validates verdict admissibility per row type (no LLM-side remapping), and that any new verdict must extend both the script vocabulary and this row in the same commit.
- §V.40 — mechanical-realization. Deterministic audit rules live exactly once, inside `scripts/check-mechanical.py`. Skill bodies and SPEC-FORMAT.md describe the contract but never restate the parser logic. This task is a pure script change because the rule is mechanical.

Related bug:

- §B.8 — the original failure: clean §I rows were genuinely classified `MATCH`, but the memo's verdict vocabulary had no slot for it, so the LLM silently remapped `MATCH → HOLD` with no documentation of the substitution. §V.43 closes the loop by making `MATCH` first-class and forbidding LLM-side remapping; §T.8 is the implementation that delivers it.

Status: complete (`x`).

Bottom line: §V.43's per-row-type verdict vocabulary — including the §I-only `MATCH` verdict — is enforced by code shipped under §T.8, eliminating the silent `MATCH → HOLD` remap that caused §B.8.

per_sample.body: x|script: write-memo `--from-audit` re-runs mechanical side internally, stdin = behavioral verdicts only; check WRITE-MEMO recipe drops hand-merge|V44,V40
per_sample.prose_minimal: When you run the `write-memo` script with the `--from-audit` flag, it internally re-runs the mechanical side of the process on its own, and the only input it takes from standard input is the behavioral verdicts. You should check the WRITE-MEMO recipe to confirm that it eliminates the hand-merge step. This applies to versions V44 and V40.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §T.9 — script: write-memo `--from-audit` re-runs mechanical side internally, stdin = behavioral verdicts only; check WRITE-MEMO recipe drops hand-merge

> T9|x|script: write-memo `--from-audit` re-runs mechanical side internally, stdin = behavioral verdicts only; check WRITE-MEMO recipe drops hand-merge|V44,V40

In plain English: this task changed how the memo file (the cached "last clean state" used by `/sdd:check`) gets written. Previously, writing the memo required hand-merging mechanical audit results with the LLM's behavioral classifications into one big table that was piped to the script. That was error-prone — the LLM could mistranscribe rows. The fix: when invoked with `--from-audit`, the `write-memo` mode re-runs all the deterministic mechanical checks itself, and the only thing it needs from stdin is the verdicts for rows that require LLM judgment (the "behavioral" ones). The `/sdd:check` recipe was then updated to drop its hand-merge step and just pass through the behavioral verdicts.

Cited invariants:

- §V.44 — the memo (`.claude/check-state.json`) is a cache, not a source of truth, and the script owns both ends: it reads the memo to issue invalidation advisories, and it writes the memo only on clean runs. The invariant explicitly says `write-memo --from-audit` re-runs mechanical rows internally so that stdin carries behavioral verdicts only, and hand-merging is banned. The LLM never decides clean and never hand-writes the memo. This row also notes exit codes: 0 clean, 1 dirty (memo untouched, so CI can gate on it), 2 invalid verdict vocab. §B.9 is closed by this row.

- §V.40 — deterministic audit rules live in exactly one place: `scripts/check-mechanical.py`. Skill bodies and SPEC-FORMAT.md describe contracts but never duplicate parser logic, and per-run regex paraphrase is banned. This is why the mechanical re-run had to happen inside the script rather than being re-implemented in the check skill.

Status: complete (`x`).

Bottom line: §V.44's "script owns both ends" rule and §V.40's "mechanical logic lives once in the script" rule are jointly enforced by giving `write-memo --from-audit` the ability to recompute mechanical rows itself, leaving the check skill to forward only behavioral verdicts.

The wrong flag was used — `disable-model-invocation: true` — which hid those skills from the Skill tool entirely. As a result, the model could no longer invoke them automatically when their triggers fired. Their slash-command surface kept working, but the whole point of an auto-fire sub-skill is to be model-invocable behind the scenes, so this defeated their purpose.

Broken invariant:
- §V.61 — auto-fire sub-skills (telegraph, backprop, socratic, steno, monitor) must declare `user-invocable: false` in frontmatter. They must never use `disable-model-invocation: true`, because that flag hides the skill from the Skill tool and so prevents the model from engaging it. `user-invocable: false` keeps the skill model-invocable while suppressing it from the operator-typed slash surface.

Date logged: 2026-06-11.

No `fix` cite points at a §T row, which means the corrective work is tracked through §V.61 itself (the tightened invariant) rather than through a dedicated remediation task in the current §T list.

Bottom line: someone reached for `disable-model-invocation` thinking it would just suppress the slash surface, but it actually hid the skill from the model too; §V.61 now pins the correct flag (`user-invocable: false`) so this can't recur.

per_sample.body: 2026-06-11|check LOAD step 1 whole-file Read + step 4 `emit-v-slices` double-loaded SPEC.md every run; large spec re-hits Read pagination cap @ step 1|V64
per_sample.prose_minimal: On June 11, 2026, a check was performed on the LOAD process, specifically looking at step 1, which performs a whole-file Read, and step 4, which is the `emit-v-slices` operation. It was found that SPEC.md was being double-loaded on every run. Additionally, when the spec is large, step 1 re-hits the Read pagination cap. This was recorded under version V64.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §B.6 — check skill double-loaded SPEC.md every run

> B6|2026-06-11|check LOAD step 1 whole-file Read + step 4 `emit-v-slices` double-loaded SPEC.md every run; large spec re-hits Read pagination cap @ step 1|V64

In plain English: the `/sdd:check` skill used to read the full `SPEC.md` file twice on every run. Its LOAD recipe step 1 did a whole-file `Read` of `SPEC.md`, and then step 4 invoked the script's `emit-v-slices` mode which also pulled the §V bodies out of the same file. That's two loads of the same content per check run. On large specs the step-1 whole-file `Read` also bumped into the `Read` tool's pagination cap, so the skill was paying a real cost (token budget + tool-call friction) for redundant data.

Bug recorded 2026-06-11.

Fix: §V.64 — single-load. That invariant says §V bodies enter run context via the script's `emit-v-slices` mode *only*, and whole-file `Read` of `SPEC.md` is banned wherever a script emit mode covers the need. Full file reads are reserved to rewrite sweeps (`/sdd:compact`, `/sdd:reorganize`) where the operator actually needs the whole document in hand.

Cited invariants:

- §V.64 — single-load. §V bodies enter run context via the script's `emit-v-slices` mode only; full-file `SPEC.md` reads are banned where an emit mode covers the need, and reserved to operator rewrite sweeps (`/sdd:compact`, `/sdd:reorganize`). This invariant exists because of this bug — the parenthetical `(closes §B.6)` on §V.64 records the link.

Related tasks (tasks citing §V.64):

- §T.4 (closed) — added the script's `emit-overview` mode, which emits §G/§C/§I/§T/§B bodies plus the §V id list (but not §V bodies). The check skill's LOAD step 1 was rewritten to call `emit-overview` instead of doing a whole-file `Read`. Combined with §V.64's rule that step 4 keeps using `emit-v-slices` for §V bodies, the double-load is gone: step 1 gets the non-§V scaffolding via `emit-overview`, step 4 gets §V bodies via `emit-v-slices`, and `SPEC.md` is never whole-file-read during a check run.

Bottom line: `/sdd:check` was reading `SPEC.md` twice per run and hitting pagination limits on large specs; §V.64 banned redundant whole-file reads and §T.4 added the `emit-overview` script mode that lets the check skill load the non-§V parts cheaply, closing the bug.

## Hint

per_sample.prose_minimal: On June 11, 2026, a batch narrow-scope override was implemented, keyed on the post-classification audit at the file-scope level. The large language model eyeballed the repository file count as a proxy. This corresponds to version V46.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §B.7 — batch narrow-scope override keyed wrong

> B7|2026-06-11|batch narrow-scope override keyed on post-classification audit file-scope → LLM eyeballed repo file count as proxy|V46

In plain English: a bug was logged on 2026-06-11 about how the §V batch-classification step decided whether to use a single agent or multiple parallel agents. The "narrow scope" shortcut (collapse to one agent when the work is small) was triggered by a file-scope number that only becomes available *after* the classification audit has already run. Because that number wasn't available at the moment the decision needed to be made, the LLM fell back to eyeballing the repository's file count as a stand-in. That proxy is not the same measurement, so the agent-count decision was driven by the wrong signal.

Broken invariant:

- §V.46 — batch sizing for §V classification must come from a specific script-emitted row (`batch|ADVISORY|recommended: <n> agents`) computed as `ceil(|V|/15)` clamped to `[1, 4]`, with a narrowing rule: if the PUBLISHED file census is less than `ceil(|V|/2)`, force 1 agent. The LLM is forbidden from hand-computing the count. The bug broke this because the narrowing rule's input (the file census) was being eyeballed rather than emitted by the script, which is exactly the hand-computation §V.46 bans.

Fixing task:

- §T.6 (status `x`, complete) — taught `scripts/check-mechanical.py` to emit the batch-advisory row directly from the §V row count and the PUBLISHED file census, and updated the check skill's batch step to consume that emitted row instead of computing a heuristic. This moves both inputs (count and census) inside the script, so the LLM no longer needs to estimate either.

Bottom line: §B.7 records that batch-agent sizing once depended on an eyeballed file count because the real census wasn't emitted yet; §T.6 closed the gap by having the script emit the full advisory row, restoring §V.46's "LLM never hand-computes" guarantee.

per_sample.body: 2026-06-11|clean §I rows classify MATCH but memo vocab lacked it → LLM silently remapped MATCH→HOLD, no doc stated mapping|V43
per_sample.prose_minimal: On June 11, 2026, during a cleanup of Section I rows, the rows were classified as MATCH, but the memo vocabulary did not include MATCH as a valid term. The LLM silently remapped MATCH to HOLD without any documentation stating that this mapping should occur. This was recorded under V43.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §B.8 — clean §I rows classify MATCH but memo vocab lacked it

> B8|2026-06-11|clean §I rows classify MATCH but memo vocab lacked it → LLM silently remapped MATCH→HOLD, no doc stated mapping|V43

In plain English: when `scripts/check-mechanical.py` audited interface (§I) rows that were clean, it produced the verdict `MATCH`. But the cached memo file (`.claude/check-state.json`) only knew about a narrower set of verdicts — `MATCH` was not in its vocabulary. Because no documentation said what should happen in that case, the LLM running the check quietly translated `MATCH` into `HOLD` (a generic silent-clean verdict). That remap was invisible: nothing in SPEC.md or any skill body declared it, so two different runs could disagree on what a clean §I row even looked like, and a reader had no way to know the translation was happening at all.

The bug was filed on 2026-06-11, in the same dated batch as B1–B12.

Cited invariant being broken:
- §V.43 — defines the drift-verdict vocabulary in three buckets: dirty (VIOLATE, UNVERIFIABLE, UNRESOLVED, TYPE-MISMATCH, DRIFT, MISSING, STALE, EXTRA), silent (HOLD, HOLD-SINCE-CLEAN, SCOPE-EMPTY, LATENT), and surfaced-clean (VIOLATE-CAPTURED). The rule is that the script — not the LLM — validates which verdicts are admissible per row type, so there is no room for LLM-side remapping. The row also requires that any new verdict extend the script vocabulary and this row in the same commit. At the time of the bug, `MATCH` was being emitted by the script but was not in V43's list, so the script could not validate it and the LLM filled the gap by remapping.

Fix landed via:
- §T.8 — closed (`x`). The task admitted `MATCH` as a clean verdict specifically on §I rows, made the script's per-row-type vocabulary validation enforce that, and added self-tests covering it. After this task, V43 explicitly names `MATCH` under §I-clean and the script rejects it on any other row type — so the silent LLM-side remap is no longer possible.

Bottom line: a verdict the script produced (`MATCH`) had no home in the documented vocabulary, so the LLM invented a silent translation; §T.8 closed the loop by adding `MATCH` to V43 as an §I-only clean verdict and making the script the sole authority on admissibility.

## Hint

§B.8 is a closed bug — its fixing task §T.8 is also closed. `run 1` walks to the next pending §T row to keep moving on live work; `run 2` reads the invariant in prose if you want to understand the verdict vocabulary as it stands now; `run 3` audits whether the fix still holds in current code.

per_sample.body: 2026-06-11|dirty run demanded full hand-merged table then refused write, exited 0 → unusable as CI gate|V44
per_sample.prose_minimal: On June 11, 2026, a dirty run required a full hand-merged table but then refused to write and exited with status 0, making it unusable as a CI gate. This was logged against version V44.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §B.9 — write-memo refused to write

> B9|2026-06-11|dirty run demanded full hand-merged table then refused write, exited 0 → unusable as CI gate|V44

In plain English: on 2026-06-11 a bug surfaced in the `write-memo` mode of `scripts/check-mechanical.py`. When the audit found drift (a "dirty" run), the script required the caller to hand-assemble the full pipe-table of row verdicts and feed it back in — but then it refused to actually write the memo file, and it exited with status `0`. Two things were wrong at once: the human/LLM workflow was forced to merge mechanical and behavioral verdicts by hand, and the `0` exit code told any CI system "everything is fine" even though drift had been detected. Net effect: the script could not be wired into a CI gate, because CI gates branch on exit code.

Fixed by §V.44:

- §V.44 — `.claude/check-state.json` is a cache, not truth. The script owns both ends of the memo: it reads the memo to produce invalidation advisories, and it writes the memo only on clean runs. `write-memo --from-audit` re-runs the mechanical rows internally, so stdin carries only behavioral verdicts and hand-merging is banned. Exit codes are now meaningful: `0` clean, `1` dirty (memo left untouched, so CI can gate on it), `2` invalid verdict vocabulary. The LLM never decides clean and never hand-writes the memo. The parenthetical `(closes §B.9)` on §V.44 records that this row is the fix.

Related task:

- §T.9 — landed the script change: `write-memo --from-audit` re-runs the mechanical side internally and the check skill's WRITE-MEMO recipe dropped the hand-merge step. Status `x` (complete).
- §T.10 — landed the exit-code contract: dirty → exit `1` with memo untouched, invalid vocab stays exit `2`, self-tests cover both. Status `x` (complete).

Status: closed bug, both fixing tasks complete.

Bottom line: §B.9 documents why the memo protocol in §V.44 exists — the previous write-memo flow couldn't gate CI and forced hand-merged tables, so the script was rebuilt to own the memo end-to-end with honest exit codes.

## Hint

## §B.10 — frontmatter grant matched command name not arg pattern

```
B10|2026-06-11|frontmatter grant matched command name not arg pattern: `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` no-expand in `allowed-tools` → broad `Bash(python3 *)` 4 skills; check carried zero-use `Bash(git *)`|V62
```

In plain English: this bug records what went wrong with the `allowed-tools` permission grants in skill frontmatter. The intent was to give skills permission to invoke a *specific* script — `python3 ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/check-mechanical.py` — by pinning the script path inside the bash pattern. That pinning never worked, because Claude Code matches `allowed-tools` against the literal command name plus argument pattern *before* expanding shell variables, and `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` does not expand inside frontmatter at all. The narrowest grant the author could write that still admitted the real invocation was `Bash(python3 *)` — i.e. *any* `python3` command with any arguments. Four skills ended up carrying that broad grant. Separately, the `check` skill was carrying a `Bash(git *)` grant that nothing in its body actually used — dead permission, never exercised.

Date: 2026-06-11.

Cited invariant:
- §V.62 — tooling preference and grant discipline: pattern scans go through `rg --pcre2`; JSON through `jq` with a `python3` fallback; the audit script is single-file stdlib-only `python3`. Frontmatter grants must be the *narrowest pattern* over the invocations the body actually prescribes — a grant with zero matching uses in the body is banned. Script-sole-use `python3` should be pinned to the script path via the mid-glob form `Bash(python3 */check-mechanical.py *)`. When the pin is inexpressible — as it is here, because `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` does not expand in frontmatter — the rule permits a broader grant accompanied by an inline note citing the upstream limitation. This closure clause exists because of §B.10.

Fixing task:
- §T.11 — sweep frontmatter grants across `skills/*/SKILL.md`, scoped by `rg -n 'Bash\(' skills/*/SKILL.md`: drop grants with zero body uses (e.g. `check`'s `Bash(git *)`); replace script-sole-use `python3` grants with the mid-glob pin in `check` and `compact`; leave the broader `python3` grants in `reorganize` and `monitor` (which legitimately fall back to `python3` from `jq`) and add an inline note. Status: complete (`x`).
- §T.12 — file an upstream feature request on `anthropics/claude-code` asking for env-var expansion in skill frontmatter `allowed-tools`, which would let the pin actually reference `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` and remove the need for the inline-note workaround. Status: complete (`x`).

Bottom line: the permission grants in skill frontmatter were broader than intended because the path-pinning form the author wanted is blocked by an upstream limitation; §V.62 was tightened to codify both the pin-when-you-can rule and the broad-grant-plus-note fallback, §T.11 swept the existing skills into compliance, and §T.12 pushed the upstream fix.

## Hint

§B.10 is closed and its fixing tasks are both complete — there is no remaining work attached to this row. Item 1 reads the tightened invariant that absorbed the lesson; item 2 reads the sweep task that applied it; item 3 audits whether the current skills still match the rule.

## Next

1. /sdd:explain §V.62 — read the invariant this bug tightened
2. /sdd:explain §T.11 — read the sweep that applied the fix
3. /sdd:check — audit whether skill frontmatter still complies with §V.62

per_sample.body: spec-adjacent-register — SPEC.md, `skills/**/SKILL.md`, SPEC-FORMAT.md, spec-referencing prose ! telegraph per telegraph skill; /sdd:explain decodes on demand.
per_sample.prose_minimal: The register known as spec-adjacent applies to a specific set of files: SPEC.md, every SKILL.md file located anywhere within the skills directory hierarchy, SPEC-FORMAT.md, and any prose that references specifications. All of these are written in a compressed, telegraphic style according to the conventions defined in the telegraph skill. When someone needs to understand this compressed content, the command `/sdd:explain` decodes it for them whenever they request it.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.1 — spec-adjacent-register

> V1: spec-adjacent-register — SPEC.md, `skills/**/SKILL.md`, SPEC-FORMAT.md, spec-referencing prose ! telegraph per telegraph skill; /sdd:explain decodes on demand.

In plain English: any document that lives next to the spec — that means `SPEC.md` itself, every skill body under `skills/**/SKILL.md`, the `SPEC-FORMAT.md` contract file, and any prose that refers back to the spec — must be written in the compressed telegraph register produced by the telegraph skill. Because that register is terse and symbol-heavy, the `/sdd:explain` command exists to decode any part of it back into ordinary prose whenever a reader asks.

Tasks that cite this invariant:
- None of the §T rows cite §V.1 directly.

Related invariants in the same register cluster:
- §V.2 — github-facing-register — the parallel rule for outward-facing text (README, issues, PRs, commit bodies), which uses the lighter "steno" register instead of full telegraph.
- §V.3 — verbatim-preservation — code, paths, identifiers, and other literal tokens are never compressed, even inside telegraphed text.
- §V.4 — symbol-set — fixes the exact symbol vocabulary the telegraph register is allowed to use.

Status: invariants carry no status cell — they are standing rules, always in force.

Bottom line: spec-adjacent files are authored in telegraph, and `/sdd:explain` (this very command) is the sanctioned way to read them in plain English on demand.

per_sample.body: github-facing-register — README, issues, PRs, commit-msg bodies ! steno per steno skill; commit subjects = per-skill fixed templates, verbatim.
per_sample.prose_minimal: The register named "github-facing-register" applies to the following materials: the README file, issues, pull requests, and the bodies of commit messages. These should be written in stenographic style, following the stenography skill. However, commit subjects are an exception: they must use the fixed templates defined per skill, reproduced verbatim.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.2 — github-facing-register

> V2: github-facing-register — README, issues, PRs, commit-msg bodies ! steno per steno skill; commit subjects = per-skill fixed templates, verbatim.

In plain English: anything the project shows on GitHub — the README file, issue text, pull-request descriptions, and the body of commit messages — must be written in the "steno" compressed register, following the rules defined by the steno skill. The one exception is the *subject line* of a commit (its first line): that is not steno-compressed. Instead, each skill uses a fixed, predefined template for its commit subjects, and that template is reproduced exactly, word for word.

So there are two distinct registers in play across the project:
- **Steno** governs GitHub-facing prose (README, issues, PRs, commit bodies).
- **Telegraph** (a separate, denser register) governs the spec-adjacent material — see the sibling invariant below.

Sibling invariant for contrast:
- §V.1 — spec-adjacent-register: SPEC.md, the SKILL.md skill bodies, SPEC-FORMAT.md, and any spec-referencing prose use the *telegraph* register instead, and `/sdd:explain` (this command) decodes telegraph on demand.

Tasks that cite §V.2:
- None. No §T row currently references this invariant.

Bugs referencing §V.2:
- None directly. Note that §B.5 records a compression-claim drift in the README (measured ~30% versus legacy "quarter"/"4x" wording), which is GitHub-facing material §V.2 governs, though that bug row cites no invariant.

Bottom line: §V.2 splits the project's writing into registers — GitHub-facing text gets steno, with commit subjects left as verbatim fixed templates — distinct from the telegraph register §V.1 assigns to spec and skill files.

per_sample.body: verbatim-preservation — code, backticked text, paths, URLs, identifiers, numbers, versions, error strings, SQL, regex, JSON, YAML, quoted strings never compressed; backtick-wrapped tokens exempt every residue + cite audit.
per_sample.prose_minimal: The principle known as verbatim-preservation establishes that certain categories of content must never be compressed. These categories include code, any text wrapped in backticks, file paths, URLs, identifiers, numbers, version designations, error strings, SQL statements, regular expressions, JSON data, YAML data, and quoted strings. Tokens that are wrapped in backticks are exempt from compression. This exemption applies to every residue, and it requires citation for audit purposes.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.3 — verbatim-preservation

> V3: verbatim-preservation — code, backticked text, paths, URLs, identifiers, numbers, versions, error strings, SQL, regex, JSON, YAML, quoted strings never compressed; backtick-wrapped tokens exempt every residue + cite audit.

In plain English: this invariant forbids the telegraph compression from ever touching content that must stay byte-exact. When the spec or skill bodies are written in telegraph shorthand, certain kinds of text are off-limits to abbreviation: source code, anything inside backticks, file paths, URLs, identifiers, raw numbers, version strings, error messages, SQL, regular expressions, JSON, YAML, and quoted strings. All of these must be reproduced verbatim. As a further consequence, any token wrapped in backticks is exempt from the residue-cleanup and citation-resolution audits — the tooling treats it as protected literal text rather than prose to scan.

Tasks citing this invariant:
- None. No §T row currently cites §V.3.

Bugs referencing this invariant:
- None. No §B row currently references §V.3.

Related invariants (same register cluster, §V.1–V.4):
- §V.1 — spec-adjacent files must be written in telegraph; `/sdd:explain` decodes on demand.
- §V.2 — github-facing text uses the steno register instead.
- §V.4 — the allowed symbol set; heavier math operators must be spelled out as ASCII words.

Status: §V rows have no status cell; invariants are always-on rules, not tasks.

Bottom line: §V.3 guarantees that literal, machine-meaningful text (code, paths, numbers, backticked tokens) is never abbreviated or audited as prose — it is preserved exactly as written.

## Hint

§V.3 is an invariant with no citing tasks or bugs, so there is nothing to build against it directly. The natural follow-ups are reading a sibling invariant in the same register cluster, or auditing whether the spec and code actually honor verbatim preservation.

per_sample.body: symbol-set — keep set `→ ≥ ≤ ! ? §` (steno adds `&`, list-`|`); heavier math operators ! ASCII words.
per_sample.prose_minimal: The symbol set that should be kept consists of the following characters: the arrow (→), the greater-than-or-equal-to sign (≥), the less-than-or-equal-to sign (≤), the exclamation mark (!), the question mark (?), and the section sign (§). When writing in shorthand or steno, two additional symbols are added to this set: the ampersand (&) and the pipe character (|), which is used for lists. However, heavier or more complex mathematical operators should not be written as symbols; instead, they should be spelled out using their ASCII word equivalents.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.4 — symbol-set

> V4: symbol-set — keep set `→ ≥ ≤ ! ? §` (steno adds `&`, list-`|`); heavier math operators ! ASCII words.

In plain English: the spec restricts which special symbols are allowed in telegraph and steno writing. The approved core set is exactly these six characters: `→`, `≥`, `≤`, `!`, `?`, and `§`. The steno register (used for GitHub-facing text) may additionally use `&`, and lists may use the pipe character `|`. Any heavier mathematical operator beyond this set must be spelled out as ordinary ASCII words instead of a symbol.

Tasks that cite this invariant:
- None. No §T row currently references §V.4.

Bugs that reference this invariant:
- None. No §B row currently points at §V.4.

This invariant sits within the §V "register" cluster (V1–V4), which governs how the two writing registers — telegraph for spec-adjacent text (§V.1) and steno for GitHub-facing text (§V.2) — are encoded, alongside the verbatim-preservation rule (§V.3) that protects code and literal tokens from compression.

Bottom line: only the symbols `→ ≥ ≤ ! ? §` (plus `&` in steno and `|` in lists) are permitted; every other math operator must be written out as an English word.

## Hint

§V.4 is a standalone invariant with no citing tasks or bugs, so the useful next steps are reading its sibling register rules (§V.1, §V.2, §V.3) or checking whether the codebase actually honors this symbol restriction.

## Next

2. /sdd:explain §V.1 — read the telegraph-register invariant that §V.4 supports
3. /sdd:check — audit whether the spec and skills obey the symbol-set rule

per_sample.body: shape-semantics-split — SPEC-FORMAT.md binds row shape + section catalog + citation grammar; §V rows bind semantics + enforcement; neither restates the other.

This document is responsible for binding together three things: the shape of a row, the catalog of sections, and the grammar used for citations. Separately, the rows in section V are responsible for binding together two different things: semantics and enforcement. Neither of these—SPEC-FORMAT.md on one side, and the section V rows on the other—repeats or restates what the other one covers.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.11 — shape-semantics-split

> V11: shape-semantics-split — SPEC-FORMAT.md binds row shape + section catalog + citation grammar; §V rows bind semantics + enforcement; neither restates the other.

In plain English: this invariant draws a clean line between two documents so they never overlap. `SPEC-FORMAT.md` is the place that defines the *form* of the spec — what a row looks like, which sections are allowed to exist, and how citations are written. The `§V` invariants in `SPEC.md` are the place that defines the *meaning* — what the rules actually require and how they are enforced. Each document owns its own half, and neither is allowed to repeat what the other already says.

- §V.13 — every citation must resolve to a real row or interface kind. This depends on the citation grammar that §V.11 assigns to `SPEC-FORMAT.md`.

Bottom line: §V.11 is a separation-of-concerns rule — `SPEC-FORMAT.md` owns structure and citation grammar, `§V` rows own meaning and enforcement, and the two must never duplicate each other.
