per_sample.body: monotonic-numbering — V/T/B ids strictly increasing in section order; gaps OK, reuse banned; sole renumber path = /sdd:reorganize (map append + cite sweep, same commit).
per_sample.prose_minimal: Monotonic numbering requires that V, T, and B identifiers are strictly increasing in section order. Gaps are allowed, but reuse is banned. The sole renumbering path is /sdd:reorganize, which performs a map append plus a cite sweep in the same commit.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.12 — monotonic-numbering

> V12: monotonic-numbering — V/T/B ids strictly increasing in section order; gaps OK, reuse banned; sole renumber path = /sdd:reorganize (map append + cite sweep, same commit).

In plain English: identifiers for invariants (§V), tasks (§T), and bugs (§B) must only ever go up as you read down the section. Numbers may skip (gaps are fine and expected after clustering or archival), but a number that has already been used must never be handed out again to a different row. The only allowed way to renumber is the `/sdd:reorganize` skill, which in one commit both appends the old-to-new mapping and rewrites every citation that pointed at the old ids.

Cited by tasks: none currently open or closed cite V12 directly.

Referenced by bugs: none.

Related invariants (for context, not direct cites):
- §V.13 (cite-resolution) — every citation must still resolve after any renumber.
- §V.15 (renumber-chain-walk) — historical ids are followed newest-first through the append-only map until they land on a live id or the archive sentinel.
- §V.16 (archive-semantics) — retired rows move verbatim to SPEC.archive.md and stay resolvable.

Status: invariant (always in force).

Bottom line: ids only increase, never reuse; the single legal renumber door is `/sdd:reorganize` with map + cite sweep in the same commit.

## Hint

V12 is structural law, not a work item. Typical follow-ups are reading the renumber/archive companions or checking whether the live SPEC still obeys monotonic ids.

## Next

1. /sdd:explain §V.15 — how historical ids walk the renumber map
2. /sdd:explain §V.13 — citation resolution rules after renumber
3. /sdd:explain --next — next pending §T row
4. /sdd:check — audit whether live V/T/B ids still obey monotonic numbering

per_sample.body: cite-resolution — every cite ! resolve: `cites` tokens → live/archived V/T/B row or live §I kind, `fix` tokens → §V row, free-text `§<S>.<n>` → §<S> row; renumber sweeps citers same commit.
per_sample.prose_minimal: Cite-resolution requires that every citation must resolve. Tokens of the cites kind resolve to a live or archived V, T, or B row or to a live section I kind. Tokens of the ref kind resolve to a section V row. Free-text references of the form section S point n resolve to a section S row. Renumbering sweeps update citers in the same commit.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.13 — cite-resolution

> V13: cite-resolution — every cite ! resolve: `cites` tokens → live/archived V/T/B row or live §I kind, `fix` tokens → §V row, free-text `§<S>.<n>` → §<S> row; renumber sweeps citers same commit.

In plain English: every citation that appears anywhere in the spec must point at something that actually exists. There are three kinds of citation tokens, each with its own resolution rule:

- Tokens in a task’s `cites` column must resolve to a live or archived §V, §T, or §B row, or to a live §I interface kind.
- Tokens in a bug’s `fix` column must resolve to a §V invariant row.
- Free-text references written as `§<S>.<n>` (for example in prose or other fields) must resolve to a real row in section §S.

When rows are renumbered, every place that cited the old numbers must be updated in the same commit so nothing is left dangling.

Tasks that cite this invariant: none currently.

Bugs that reference this invariant: none currently.

Closely related invariants (for context):
- §V.12 — ids only ever increase; the only legal renumber path is `/sdd:reorganize`.
- §V.14 — published text may not contain placeholder or pinned digit cites.
- §V.15 — historical ids walk an append-only renumber map to a live id or an archive sentinel.
- §V.16 — archived rows stay cite-resolvable forever and are never edited.

Status: active invariant (no status column on §V rows).

Bottom line: no citation may dangle; every cite token must resolve, and renumbers must fix all citers in the same commit.

## Hint

No open §T row implements or audits this invariant directly. Typical next steps are to inspect a related numbering/cite rule or to move to the next pending task.

## Next

1. /sdd:explain §V.12 — read the monotonic-numbering rule that feeds renumber
2. /sdd:explain §V.15 — read how historical ids walk the renumber map
3. /sdd:explain --next — expand the next pending §T row
4. /sdd:check — audit whether any live cites currently fail to resolve

per_sample.body: pinned-cite-ban — PUBLISHED bodies ! placeholder (`§V.<n>`) or named-invariant form, never pinned §-digit cites; SPEC.md-narrative + REPO-LOCAL pinned cites ! resolve live.
per_sample.prose_minimal: Pinned-cite-ban means that published bodies must not use placeholder citations in the form §V.<n> or named-invariant form, and must never use pinned section-digit cites; SPEC.md narrative and repo-local pinned cites must not resolve live.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.14 — pinned-cite-ban

> V14: pinned-cite-ban — PUBLISHED bodies ! placeholder (`§V.<n>`) or named-invariant form, never pinned §-digit cites; SPEC.md-narrative + REPO-LOCAL pinned cites ! resolve live.

In plain English: this invariant bans hard-coded numeric section citations in anything that ships to the public. Published bodies (README, issues, PRs, commit messages, and other outward-facing prose) may only refer to invariants by the placeholder form `§V.<n>` or by the invariant’s stable name (for example “pinned-cite-ban”). They must never embed a concrete digit cite such as `§V.14`, because renumbering would silently break those links. Inside SPEC.md itself and in repo-local files under `.spec/`, pinned numeric cites are allowed, but every one of them must still resolve to a currently live row.

Related invariants:
- §V.12 — monotonic-numbering: ids only increase; the sole renumber path is `/sdd:reorganize`, which is exactly why published text cannot pin digits.
- §V.13 — cite-resolution: every cite token must resolve to a live or archived row; V14 specializes that rule for the published-versus-internal split.
- §V.15 — renumber-chain-walk: the append-only renumber map lets historical ids walk forward to a live id or the `archive` sentinel, which is what keeps internal pinned cites honest after a renumber.

Cited by tasks: none currently open or closed list V14 in their `cites` field.

Cited by bugs: none.

Bottom line: public prose stays renumber-safe by using names or placeholders; only SPEC.md and `.spec/` may pin digits, and those pins must still point at live rows.

## Hint

V14 is an always-on invariant, not a task. If you want to see how renumbering actually rewrites cites, read V12/V15 next; if you want to verify the rule still holds in the tree, run a check.

## Next

1. /sdd:explain §V.12 — read the monotonic-numbering rule that forces this ban
2. /sdd:explain §V.15 — read how historical pins walk the renumber map
3. /sdd:check — audit whether any published body currently embeds pinned digit cites
4. /sdd:explain --next — move to the lowest-numbered pending §T row

per_sample.body: renumber-chain-walk — `.spec/spec-renumber-map.json` append-only; historical id resolves newest-first to live id or `archive` sentinel (→ SPEC.archive.md §V.retired block, never live row).
per_sample.prose_minimal: Renumber-chain-walk uses the append-only file `.spec/spec-renumber-map.json`. A historical id is resolved newest-first to either a live id or the `archive` sentinel, which points to the §V.retired block in SPEC.archive.md and never to a live row.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.15 — renumber-chain-walk

> V15: renumber-chain-walk — `.spec/spec-renumber-map.json` append-only; historical id resolves newest-first to live id or `archive` sentinel (→ SPEC.archive.md §V.retired block, never live row).

In plain English: whenever an invariant, task, or bug id is renumbered, the old id is never erased from history. Instead an append-only map file (`.spec/spec-renumber-map.json`) records every old-to-new mapping. Anyone who still holds a historical citation walks that chain from newest entry backward until they land on either the current live id in SPEC.md or the special sentinel `archive`. The archive sentinel means the row has been retired and now lives only inside SPEC.archive.md under the §V.retired block; it must never be treated as a live row again.

Cited by tasks:
- §T.44 — move spec-owned files from `.claude/` to `.spec/` (already complete). That move placed the renumber map under the new `.spec/` location that this invariant names.

Referenced by bugs:
- None directly.

Related invariants that give this rule its force:
- §V.12 — ids are strictly monotonic; the only legal renumber path is `/sdd:reorganize`, which must append to the map and sweep all citers in the same commit.
- §V.13 — every citation must resolve; the chain walk is how historical cites still satisfy that rule after a renumber.
- §V.16 — archived or retired rows migrate verbatim to SPEC.archive.md and stay cite-resolvable forever.

Bottom line: old citations never break; you always walk the append-only renumber map newest-first until you hit a live id or the archive sentinel.

## Hint

§V.15 is a permanent resolution rule, not a task. The map file itself is read-only for explain; only `/sdd:reorganize` is allowed to append to it.

## Next

1. /sdd:explain §V.12 — read the monotonic-numbering rule that authorizes renumbers
2. /sdd:explain §V.16 — read how archived rows stay cite-resolvable
3. /sdd:explain §T.44 — see the completed task that relocated the map under `.spec/`
4. /sdd:explain --next — move to the next pending task

per_sample.prose_minimal: Documents that sit alongside formal specifications—such as SPEC.md, the SKILL.md files found throughout the skills directory structure, SPEC-FORMAT.md, and any prose that references or discusses specifications—should be written in a condensed, telegraphic style, following the conventions described in the telegraph skill. Since `.spec/spec-renumber-map.json` isn't available in this read, I'll resolve `§V.1` directly against the current SPEC.md. That gives us **V1: spec-adjacent-register**.

## §V.1 — spec-adjacent-register

> V1: spec-adjacent-register — SPEC.md, `skills/**/SKILL.md`, SPEC-FORMAT.md, spec-referencing prose ! telegraph per telegraph skill; /sdd:explain decodes on demand.

In plain English: this invariant says that certain files — SPEC.md itself, every skill's `SKILL.md`, `SPEC-FORMAT.md`, and any prose that directly references the spec — must be written in the compressed "telegraph" style (defined by the telegraph skill), not full prose. The `!` means this is mandatory, not optional. The whole point of writing these files in telegraph is token efficiency; the `/sdd:explain` command exists specifically to decompress that telegraph back into readable English whenever a human needs it — which is exactly what's happening right now.

Cited siblings: none directly cite `§V.1` by id in this SPEC.md snapshot. No `§T` row lists `V1` in its `cites` column, and no `§B` row lists it in its `fix` column. This invariant currently stands unreferenced by any tracked task or bug — it's foundational/structural rather than tied to a specific piece of work.

Cited interfaces: none. `§V.1` doesn't name a specific `§I` row; it's a cross-cutting rule about file register, not a single component contract.

Status: this is a `§V` (invariant) row, not a `§T` (task) row, so it doesn't carry a `.`/`x` status marker — invariants are always "live" until formally retired via the renumber/archive mechanism (`§V.15`).

Bottom line: `§V.1` mandates that all spec-adjacent files stay in telegraph form, and `/sdd:explain` is the sanctioned way to read them in prose without violating that rule.

per_sample.body: github-facing-register — README, issues, PRs, commit-msg bodies ! steno per steno skill; commit subjects = per-skill fixed templates, verbatim.
per_sample.prose_minimal:
Here is the passage rewritten in plain English while keeping every fact intact:

This refers to the register used when writing content that faces GitHub — meaning README files, issues, pull requests, and the body text of commit messages. In this context, writing is done in shorthand (steno), and the level of shorthand used depends on the specific skill being applied at the time — in other words, shorthand usage is handled on a per-skill basis. However, this is different for commit subject lines: for commit subjects, each skill has its own fixed, predetermined template, and that template must be used exactly as written, with no changes or paraphrasing (verbatim).
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.2 — github-facing-register

> V2: github-facing-register — README, issues, PRs, commit-msg bodies ! steno per steno skill; commit subjects = per-skill fixed templates, verbatim.

In plain English: any prose that lives on GitHub's surface — the README file, issue text, pull request descriptions, and the body of commit messages — must be written in "steno" register, following the rules defined by the steno skill, rather than in the telegraph shorthand used inside SPEC.md itself. The one exception is commit subject lines: those follow fixed templates defined per-skill, and must be reproduced exactly as written, with no paraphrasing or steno compression applied.

So there are effectively two writing registers in this plugin: telegraph (terse, symbol-heavy, for SPEC.md and other spec-adjacent files) and steno (a different, GitHub-appropriate compression) for anything a human on GitHub will read. Commit subjects sit outside both — they're literal, fixed strings.

Cited siblings:

Tasks citing §V.2:
- §T.40 — initializes `skills/github`, a passive gh-CLI workflow governor, citing V69, V61, V2, V41. This task presumably enforces or supports the steno-register requirement for GitHub-facing content as part of building out GitHub workflow tooling.
- §T.45 — re-anchors the steno register and the clarity carrier on simple technical language, citing V67, V2. This task revisits and tightens how V2's steno rule is applied, aligning it with the human-clarity invariant (V67).

No bugs currently reference §V.2 in the fix column.

No §I interfaces are cited by this row (§V.2 doesn't reference §I directly; it names an external skill — steno — as its enforcement mechanism, not an interface contract).

Bottom line: anything written for GitHub (README, issues, PRs, commit bodies) must be in steno register per the steno skill; commit subject lines alone are exempt, following fixed per-skill templates verbatim.

## §V.10 — sole-source-of-truth

> V10: sole-source-of-truth — SPEC.md @ repo root is sole live spec; no docs/ tree, no sidecars; SPEC.archive.md sibling carries immutable archived rows only.

In plain English: SPEC.md, sitting at the root of the repository, is the one and only living specification document. There is no `docs/` folder holding parallel spec content, and no other sidecar files split off pieces of the spec. The one exception is `SPEC.archive.md`, which lives next to SPEC.md and holds rows that have been retired — but that archive file is immutable: once a row lands there, it's frozen and never edited again.

Cited siblings found by scanning §T and §B for `V10`:

This invariant works alongside a related one already in the spec:

- §V.11 (shape-semantics-split) — draws the line between SPEC-FORMAT.md (which owns row shape and section catalog) and §V rows (which own semantics and enforcement), so neither file duplicates the other's job.
- §V.15 (renumber-chain-walk) — explains how a historical id resolves through the renumber map to either a live row or the `archive` sentinel, which is what sends a row into SPEC.archive.md in the first place.
- §V.16 (archive-semantics) — describes exactly what happens to a row once it's archived: verbatim migration, per-section markers, permanently cite-resolvable but never edited again.

Status: enforced, standing invariant (no status column — invariants don't carry `.`/`x`, they simply hold).

Bottom line: SPEC.md is the single authoritative spec file; nothing else — not a docs tree, not scattered sidecars — competes with it, and the only satellite file (SPEC.archive.md) is read-only history, not a second source of truth.

per_sample.body: shape-semantics-split — SPEC-FORMAT.md binds row shape + section catalog + citation grammar; §V rows bind semantics + enforcement; neither restates the other.
per_sample.prose_minimal:
The project separates the specification into two distinct documents (or document parts) that work together without duplicating each other's content. 

The file named SPEC-FORMAT.md is responsible for defining the structural aspects of the specification: it establishes the shape or layout that rows must follow, it defines the catalog of sections that make up the specification, and it sets out the grammar rules for how citations should be written.

Separately, the rows found in section V (§V) are responsible for defining the semantics — that is, the meaning — associated with each row, as well as how that meaning is enforced.

Importantly, these two parts do not duplicate one another: SPEC-FORMAT.md does not restate the semantic or enforcement information that lives in the §V rows, and the §V rows do not restate the structural, section-catalog, or citation-grammar rules that live in SPEC-FORMAT.md. Each document or section owns its own distinct kind of information, and the two are kept separate rather than repeating each other's content.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
Reading SPEC.md — resolving `§V.11`.

No renumber map noted as active, so resolving directly against current SPEC.md: `§V.11` → row `V11: shape-semantics-split`.

## §V.11 — shape-semantics-split

> V11: shape-semantics-split — SPEC-FORMAT.md binds row shape + section catalog + citation grammar; §V rows bind semantics + enforcement; neither restates the other.

In plain English: this invariant draws a firm line between two documents that could easily blur together. SPEC-FORMAT.md is responsible for the *mechanics* of the spec — what a row looks like, which sections exist, and how citations are written and parsed. The §V rows themselves (like this one) are responsible for the *meaning* — what must be true, and what enforces it. The rule forbids either document from duplicating the other's job: SPEC-FORMAT.md should never explain why a rule exists, and a §V row should never redefine row syntax. If you find yourself writing shape rules inside a §V row, or semantic justification inside SPEC-FORMAT.md, that's a violation of this invariant.

Cited siblings:

- No §T rows currently cite `V11` directly — no open or closed tasks reference it.
- No §B rows reference `V11` — no bug has been logged against this split breaking down.
- No §I interfaces name it (SPEC-FORMAT.md is mentioned as a format contract loaded by several skills, but not tied to this row via citation).

This invariant is currently unenforced by any script mode or task — it functions as a standing structural rule with no dedicated mechanical audit.

Status: enforced by convention (no `.`/`x` status — §V rows are standing invariants, not tasks).

Bottom line: keep SPEC-FORMAT.md and SPEC.md's §V rows non-overlapping — one governs shape, the other governs meaning, and neither should restate the other's content.

## Hint

2. /sdd:explain §G — read the top-level goal this structural rule serves

per_sample.body: monotonic-numbering — V/T/B ids strictly increasing in section order; gaps OK, reuse banned; sole renumber path = /sdd:reorganize (map append + cite sweep, same commit).
per_sample.prose_minimal: Identifiers for verses (V), terms (T), and blocks (B) must be assigned numbers that strictly increase according to the order of the sections in which they appear. It is acceptable for there to be gaps in the numbering sequence, but a number that has already been used must never be reused for something else. The only permitted way to renumber these identifiers is through the /sdd:reorganize process, which involves appending to the mapping and sweeping through all citations to update them, with both of these changes made together in the same commit.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
Resolving `§V.12` against SPEC.md.

## §V.12 — monotonic-numbering

> V12: monotonic-numbering — V/T/B ids strictly increasing in section order; gaps OK, reuse banned; sole renumber path = /sdd:reorganize (map append + cite sweep, same commit).

In plain English: the numeric ids in the §V, §T, and §B sections must always go up as you read down the section — never repeat, never go backwards. Skipping numbers is fine (that just means something was closed or retired), but reusing a number that was ever assigned is forbidden. The only way ids are ever allowed to be renumbered is through the `/sdd:reorganize` skill, and when that happens, the renumbering and the sweep of every citation that pointed at the old number must land in the same commit — never split across two commits.

Cited siblings:

- No `§T` rows cite `§V.12` directly.
- No `§B` rows reference `§V.12` directly.
- Related mechanism: `§V.15` (renumber-chain-walk) governs how a historical id, once it's been superseded by a reorganize pass, gets resolved forward — it's the runtime-lookup counterpart to the write-time guarantee `§V.12` makes.
- Related mechanism: `§V.13` (cite-resolution) requires that a renumber sweep update every citer in the same commit — this is the enforcement mechanism `§V.12` depends on to keep citations valid across a renumber.

Status: enforced (`§V` rows carry no status column — invariants are permanently live, not task-tracked).

Bottom line: id numbers in §V/§T/§B are a monotonic, append-only sequence; the only legal way to change one is a same-commit reorganize pass that also fixes every citation pointing at it.

## Hint

`§V.12` is an invariant row, not a task — there's nothing to "start." The natural next moves are either checking the plugin still honors it (drift check) or reading the two mechanisms it leans on (`§V.13`, `§V.15`) for full context.

## Next

2. /sdd:explain §V.15 — read the renumber-chain-walk mechanism this invariant depends on
3. /sdd:explain §V.13 — read the cite-resolution mechanism this invariant depends on

per_sample.body: cite-resolution — every cite ! resolve: `cites` tokens → live/archived V/T/B row or live §I kind, `fix` tokens → §V row, free-text `§<S>.<n>` → §<S> row; renumber sweeps citers same commit.
per_sample.prose_minimal:
Here is the plain-English expansion of this citation-resolution rule:

Every citation must be resolved—no exceptions. Specifically:

- Any token written as `cites` must be resolved to point to either a live or archived row in Section V, Section T, or Section B, or alternatively to a live kind (type/category) listed in Section I.
- Any token written as `fix` must be resolved to point to a row in Section V.
- Any free-text reference formatted like `§<S>.<n>` (that is, a section symbol followed by a section identifier, a period, and a number) must be resolved to point to the corresponding row in that section (Section S).
- Whenever renumbering occurs, the sweep that performs the renumbering must also update (sweep along) all citers—that is, everything that cites the renumbered item—within that same commit, so nothing is left referencing outdated numbers.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.13 — cite-resolution

> V13: cite-resolution — every cite ! resolve: `cites` tokens → live/archived V/T/B row or live §I kind, `fix` tokens → §V row, free-text `§<S>.<n>` → §<S> row; renumber sweeps citers same commit.

In plain English: this invariant guarantees that every citation anywhere in the spec system points to something real and findable. There are three kinds of citation, each with its own resolution rule:

1. **`cites` tokens** (the kind used in §T rows, e.g. `V1,I.api`) must resolve to either a live row, an archived row, or a live §I interface kind. They are never allowed to dangle.
2. **`fix` tokens** (used in §B bug rows) must resolve specifically to a §V invariant row — a bug's fix always points back to the rule it violated.
3. **Free-text citations** written in prose as `§<Section>.<n>` (like `§V.13` itself) must resolve to the matching row in that section.

The invariant also commits to upkeep: whenever a row gets renumbered, every place that cites the old number is swept and updated in the same commit as the renumber. Nothing is left pointing at a stale id.

Cited siblings:
- No §T tasks currently cite `V13` directly.
- No §B bugs currently cite `V13` as a fix.

This invariant is a structural, self-referential rule about the citation system as a whole rather than a feature invariant tied to specific tasks — it's what makes commands like `/sdd:explain` and `/sdd:check` trustworthy when they follow a cite.

Status: enforced (no `.spec/check-extras.md` stub — full body lives in SPEC.md).

Bottom line: every citation in the spec — whether a `cites` token, a `fix` token, or free prose — must resolve to a real row, and renumbering never breaks that promise.

## Hint

§V.13 is a cross-cutting integrity rule with no direct §T/§B citers, so there's nothing further to drill into here — the natural next step is either auditing the live spec for compliance or reading a related invariant like §V.12 (monotonic-numbering) or §V.15 (renumber-chain-walk), which govern the renumbering process this rule depends on.

## Next

1. /sdd:check — audit whether all live citations currently resolve
2. /sdd:explain §V.15 — read the renumber-chain-walk invariant this rule depends on

per_sample.body: pinned-cite-ban — PUBLISHED bodies ! placeholder (`§V.<n>`) or named-invariant form, never pinned §-digit cites; SPEC.md-narrative + REPO-LOCAL pinned cites ! resolve live.
per_sample.prose_minimal:
This rule concerns a ban on pinned citations, and it applies to published bodies of work.

In published documents, when referring to a section, one must never use a pinned citation with a specific section digit or number (such as citing "§3" or "§4.2" directly). Instead, published material must use either a placeholder in the form "§V.<n>" (where n represents a variable number to be filled in) or refer to the section by its named invariant (that is, using a descriptive name that stays constant rather than a number that might change).

However, this restriction does not apply to narrative content within SPEC.md or to citations that are local to the repository. In those contexts, pinned citations are permitted, but they must be resolved live — meaning the actual section numbers or references should be checked and confirmed against the current, up-to-date state of the document at the time they are used, rather than being fixed or hardcoded permanently.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
Let me load the spec context and expand §V.14.

## §V.14 — pinned-cite-ban

> V14: pinned-cite-ban — PUBLISHED bodies ! placeholder (`§V.<n>`) or named-invariant form, never pinned §-digit cites; SPEC.md-narrative + REPO-LOCAL pinned cites ! resolve live.

In plain English: this rule splits citation style by audience. Anything written for a PUBLISHED, external-facing surface — things like README text, commit messages, issue/PR bodies, or other GitHub-facing prose — must never contain a hard-pinned citation like `§V14` or `§V.14`. Instead it must either use a generic placeholder form (literally `§V.<n>`) or refer to the invariant by its name (e.g. "pinned-cite-ban"). The reasoning: published bodies live forever in public history, but §V row numbers can shift when `/sdd:reorganize` renumbers things. A pinned digit citation in a GitHub issue from six months ago would silently go stale and point at the wrong invariant.

The second half of the rule covers the opposite case: citations that live inside SPEC.md itself (its own narrative text) or in REPO-LOCAL files (things like `.spec/check-extras.md`) — these *are* allowed to use pinned digit citations, but with an obligation: they must always resolve to a currently-live row. If a renumber happens, these pinned cites are expected to get swept and corrected in the same commit (this is what §V.13 and §V.15's renumber-chain-walk backstop).

So the split is: **PUBLISHED = never pin, REPO-LOCAL/SPEC.md-narrative = pin but keep live**.

Cited siblings — no §T row currently cites §V14 directly, and no §I row is cited by it. It does have a related bug:

- §B14 (2026-06-13) — a concrete violation of the *spirit* of this area: a Next-block wrongly named `/sdd:backprop` as something the user could directly dispatch, when the real route was `/sdd:spec`. That bug is tagged against §V24 (response-shape), not §V14 directly, but it's illustrative of the same family of problem — stale/wrong references leaking into user-facing output.

No §V-row cites §V14 as a fold target, and no open §T task is currently assigned to enforce it further — it appears to be a settled, already-integrated invariant (most of the plugin's PUBLISHED-facing sweeps, like T31/T34/T37, were about symbols and idiom rather than this specific pinned-cite concern).

Bottom line: never write a raw `§V14`-style digit citation into a README, commit message, issue, or PR — use `§V.<n>` as a placeholder or the invariant's name instead; pinned digit citations are reserved for SPEC.md's own narrative and REPO-LOCAL files, and even there they must be kept live across renumbers.

## Next

1. /sdd:explain §V13 — read the cite-resolution invariant this rule depends on
2. /sdd:check — audit whether any PUBLISHED-facing surface currently violates pinned-cite-ban

per_sample.body: renumber-chain-walk — `.spec/spec-renumber-map.json` append-only; historical id resolves newest-first to live id or `archive` sentinel (→ SPEC.archive.md §V.retired block, never live row).
per_sample.prose_minimal:
The system uses a mechanism called renumber-chain-walk, which relies on a file located at `.spec/spec-renumber-map.json`. This file is append-only, meaning new entries can be added to it but existing entries are never modified or removed.

When resolving a historical identifier, the system searches through the map starting from the newest entries and working backward toward the oldest (newest-first order). This resolution process determines whether the historical id maps to a currently live identifier, or whether it maps to the special "archive" sentinel value.

If the historical id resolves to the archive sentinel, that means it points to the retired block found in section V of the file SPEC.archive.md (referred to as SPEC.archive.md §V.retired block). Importantly, in this archive case, the identifier must never be resolved to or associated with a live row—it only ever points to the archived, retired record.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §V.15 — renumber-chain-walk

> V15: renumber-chain-walk — `.spec/spec-renumber-map.json` append-only; historical id resolves newest-first to live id or `archive` sentinel (→ SPEC.archive.md §V.retired block, never live row).

In plain English: when a §V invariant gets renumbered, the plugin never edits old citations in place.
Instead it appends a mapping entry (old id → new id) to `.spec/spec-renumber-map.json`, and that file only ever grows — nothing in it is ever rewritten or deleted.

If someone (or some skill) later looks up an old, superseded id, the resolution process walks the map starting from the newest entries first, hopping forward through however many renumbers happened, until it lands on either:

- a currently-live §V row in SPEC.md, or
- the special `archive` sentinel, which means the id was retired rather than renamed — in that case the real content lives in SPEC.archive.md under a `§V.retired` block, not as a live row anywhere.

This is what lets other skills (like `explain` itself, per its own LOAD step) safely resolve a citation like `§V.7` even if that invariant was renumbered three times since the citation was written — without ever having to rewrite historical citations across the whole repo.

Cited siblings: none — no live §T row or §B row currently cites §V.15 directly.

Related invariants worth knowing alongside this one:
- §V.12 (monotonic-numbering) — establishes that renumbering only happens through `/sdd:reorganize`, which is the sole writer of this map.
- §V.16 (archive-semantics) — defines what "archived" actually means for a retired row (verbatim migration to SPEC.archive.md, never edited afterward).
- §V.13 (cite-resolution) — the general citation-resolution contract that §V.15 specializes for the renumber case.

Status: this is a permanent, always-enforced invariant — it has no `.`/`x` status; it's structural.

Bottom line: §V.15 guarantees old §V citations never go stale silently — they either resolve forward to a live row or land cleanly on the archive.

## Hint

This invariant is infrastructural — no open task or bug currently references it, so there's nothing to build. The natural next moves are either reading a related invariant or checking the map's actual state.

## Next

1. /sdd:explain §V.16 — read the archive-semantics invariant this one hands off to
2. /sdd:check — verify the renumber map + SPEC.md are currently consistent

per_sample.body: x|add CI: script --self-test + audit dirty-verdict gate on push/PR|V40,V62

per_sample.prose_minimal: Attempting to write a memo marked as "dirty" using the x-script utility resulted in a failure. per_sample.prose_canonical:
## §T.10 — script write-memo dirty → exit 1; self-test exit codes

> T10|x|script write-memo --from-audit; check drops hand-merge|V44,V40

In plain English: this task changed the `check-mechanical.py` script so that when the write-memo mode produces a "dirty" result (meaning the memo it would write disagrees with, or is out of sync with, the current audit state), the script exits with status code 1 instead of exiting cleanly. It also extended the script's self-test suite to verify that these exit codes are correct — that clean runs exit 0 and dirty runs exit 1. This makes the script usable as a real gate (for example in CI) rather than something that always reports success regardless of outcome.

Cited invariants:

- §V44 — memo: governs the shape and lifecycle of the write-memo mode, including how a memo is produced from an audit and what "dirty" means for that memo. (Full row body lives in `.spec/check-extras.md §V44`, not inline in SPEC.md.)
- §V40 — mechanical-realization: governs when a check or rule should be pushed down into the deterministic script rather than re-implemented as an ad hoc LLM judgment call each time. (Full row body lives in `.spec/check-extras.md §V40`.)

Note: both cited invariants are stub rows in SPEC.md pointing to `.spec/check-extras.md` for their full text — I don't have access to that file's contents here, so I can't expand their bodies further. Only the summary label of each is available.

Related bugs:

- §B9 — the bug this task fixes directly: "dirty write-memo demanded full table then exit 0 → unusable as CI gate." Before this task, a dirty memo state still exited 0 (success), which made it impossible to use write-memo as an automated gate — a CI system checking the exit code would never see a failure even when the memo was dirty.

Status: complete (`x`).

Bottom line: write-memo now signals failure (exit 1) on a dirty result and self-tests confirm both exit-code paths, closing the gap described in §B9 and fulfilling the CI-gate requirement referenced by §T1.

per_sample.body: 2026-06-11|§I id derivation hardcoded dev-repo slash-bullets → zero ids in consumers|V41
per_sample.prose_minimal: On June 11, 2026, an issue was identified in section I concerning ID derivation: the process for generating identifiers was hardcoded to a development repository's slash-bullet formatting convention. As a result, this hardcoding caused zero IDs to be produced or recognized in the downstream consumer systems. This finding is recorded under version V41.
per_sample.prose_canonical:
Reading the row §B.3 from SPEC.md.

## §B.3 — §I id derivation hardcoded dev-repo slash-bullets

> B3|2026-06-11|§I id derivation hardcoded dev-repo slash-bullets → zero ids in consumers|V41

In plain English: on 2026-06-11, a bug was found where the logic for deriving `§I` (interface) row ids assumed a specific dev-repo formatting convention — bulleted lines starting with `/` (like the slash-command bullets you see in this very SPEC.md's §I section). That assumption worked fine in the plugin's own dev repo, but when the same logic ran in a consumer repo (a repo that installs and uses this plugin, rather than the plugin's home repo), the interface bullets weren't shaped the same way. The id-derivation step found nothing to match, so it produced zero `§I` ids — silently breaking interface citation in every consuming repo.

The fix is tied to invariant §V41.

Cited invariants:

- §V41: parametric-recipe. B9|2026-06-11|dirty write-memo demanded full table then exit 0 → unusable as CI gate|V44

In plain English: this bug was logged on 2026-06-11. When `write-memo` was run against a dirty state (drift present), it required a fully-populated verdict table before it would proceed, but then it exited with status 0 (success) anyway. That combination made the mode useless as a CI gate, because a CI job checks the exit code to decide pass/fail — and this mode always reported success even when it had just demanded remediation, so a broken build would never actually fail CI.
